subject: Physics
q:
A monoatomic gas performs a work of Q4 where Q is the heat supplied to it. The molar heat capacity of the gas will be _____ R during this transformation.

Where R is the gas constant.
question:
A monoatomic gas performs a work of Q4 where Q is the heat supplied to it. The molar heat capacity of the gas will be _____ R during this transformation.

Where R is the gas constant.
solution:
Using first law of thermodynamics,

Q=∆U+W

⇒Q=ΔU+Q4

⇒ΔU=3Q4

⇒∆U=32nRΔT=3Q4

Therefore, molar heat capacity, C=Qn∆T

⇒C=2R

subject: Physics
q: The volume of an ideal gas increases 8 times and temperature becomes $(1 / 4)^{\text {th }}$ of initial temperature during a reversible change. If there is no exchange of heat in this process $(\Delta \mathrm{Q}=0)$ then identify the gas from the following options (Assuming the gases given in the options are ideal gases):
question: The volume of an ideal gas increases 8 times and temperature becomes $(1 / 4)^{\text {th }}$ of initial temperature during a reversible change. If there is no exchange of heat in this process $(\Delta \mathrm{Q}=0)$ then identify the gas from the following options (Assuming the gases given in the options are ideal gases):
solution:
For an adiabatic process ($\Delta Q = 0$) with an ideal gas: $TV^{\gamma-1} = \text{constant}$
Given: $V_f = 8V_i$ and $T_f = \frac{1}{4}T_i$
Applying the adiabatic relation:
$T_i V_i^{\gamma-1} = T_f V_f^{\gamma-1}$
$T_i V_i^{\gamma-1} = \frac{T_i}{4}(8V_i)^{\gamma-1}$
$1 = \frac{1}{4} \cdot 8^{\gamma-1}$
$4 = 8^{\gamma-1}$
$2^2 = 2^{3(\gamma-1)}$
$2 = 3(\gamma-1)$
$\gamma = \frac{5}{3} = 1.67$
This value corresponds to a monatomic gas. Among the options, only He is monatomic ($\gamma = 5/3$ for monatomic ideal gases). O₂ and N₂ are diatomic ($\gamma = 7/5$), CO₂ is polyatomic ($\gamma \approx 1.30$), and NH₃ is polyatomic.

solution:
f1n1RT12+f2n2RT22

=3×52RT+52×3RT=15

subject: Physics
q: One gas of $n_1$ mole of molecules at temperature $T_1$, volume $V_1$, and pressure $P_1$, and another gas of $n_2$ mole of molecules at temperature $T_2$, volume $V_2$, and pressure $P_2$, are mixed resulting in pressure $P$ and volume $V$ of the mixture. The temperature of the mixture is _____.
question: One gas of $n_1$ mole of molecules at temperature $T_1$, volume $V_1$, and pressure $P_1$, and another gas of $n_2$ mole of molecules at temperature $T_2$, volume $V_2$, and pressure $P_2$, are mixed resulting in pressure $P$ and volume $V$ of the mixture. The temperature of the mixture is _____.
solution:
From the ideal gas equation, the number of moles of the first gas is $n_1 = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{R T_1}$ and the number of moles of the second gas is $n_2 = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{R T_2}$.

For the mixture, the total number of moles is $n = n_1 + n_2$.

Applying the ideal gas equation for the mixture:

$P V = n R T$

$T = \dfrac{P V}{n R}$

Substituting the values of $n_1$ and $n_2$:

$T = \dfrac{P V}{\left( \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{R T_1} + \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{R T_2} \right) R}$

$T = \dfrac{P V}{\dfrac{P_1 V_1}{T_1} + \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}}$

$T = \dfrac{T_1 T_2 P V}{P_1 V_1 T_2 + P_2 V_2 T_1}$

Answer: $T_1 T_2 PV/(T_2 P_1 V_1 + T_1 P_2 V_2)$

subject: Physics
q: The specific heat at constant pressure of a real gas obeying $P V^2=R T$ equation is:
question: The specific heat at constant pressure of a real gas obeying $P V^2=R T$ equation is:
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{dQ}=\mathrm{du}+\mathrm{dW} \\ & \mathrm{CdT}=\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}} \mathrm{dT}+\mathrm{PdV} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{PV}^2=\mathrm{RT} \\ & \mathrm{P}=\text { constant } \\ & \mathrm{P}(2 \mathrm{VdV})=\mathrm{RdT} \\ & \mathrm{PdV}=\frac{\mathrm{RdT}}{2 \mathrm{~V}} \\ & \end{aligned}$
Put in equation (1) $\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}+\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2 \mathrm{~V}}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: In a diatomic molecule, the rotational energy at a given temperature obeys Maxwell's distribution.

Statement II : In a diatomic molecule, the rotational energy at a given temperature equals the translational kinetic energy for each molecule.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: In a diatomic molecule, the rotational energy at a given temperature obeys Maxwell's distribution.

Statement II : In a diatomic molecule, the rotational energy at a given temperature equals the translational kinetic energy for each molecule.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
options: Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is false but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is true.
Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> is true but Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> is false.
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are true.
Both Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">I</mi></math> and Statement <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>II</mi></math> are false.
solution:
The translational kinetic energy & rotational kinetic energy both obey Maxwell's distribution
independent of each other.
Translational kinetic energy of diatomic molecules =32kT
Rotational kinetic energy of diatomic molecules =22kT
So statement I is true but statement II is false.

q: A closed vessel contains <math><mrow><mn>0.1</mn></mrow></math> mole of a monoatomic ideal gas at <math><mrow><mn>200</mn><mtext> K</mtext></mrow></math>. If <math><mrow><mn>0.05</mn></mrow></math> mole of the same gas at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is added to it, the final equilibrium temperature (in <math><mtext>K</mtext></math>) of the gas in the vessel will be close to ______
question: A closed vessel contains <math><mrow><mn>0.1</mn></mrow></math> mole of a monoatomic ideal gas at <math><mrow><mn>200</mn><mtext> K</mtext></mrow></math>. If <math><mrow><mn>0.05</mn></mrow></math> mole of the same gas at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>400</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></math> is added to it, the final equilibrium temperature (in <math><mtext>K</mtext></math>) of the gas in the vessel will be close to ______
solution:
0.1 200+0.05 400=0.15T

T=20+200.15=8003=266.67

=266.67

subject: Physics
q: A mixture of one mole of monoatomic gas and one mole of a diatomic gas (rigid) are kept at room temperature $\left(27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$. The ratio of specific heat of gases at constant volume respectively is:
question: A mixture of one mole of monoatomic gas and one mole of a diatomic gas (rigid) are kept at room temperature $\left(27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$. The ratio of specific heat of gases at constant volume respectively is:
solution: $\frac{\left(\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}\right)_{\text {mono }}}{\left(\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{v}}\right)_{\mathrm{dia}}}=\frac{\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{R}}{\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{R}}=\frac{3}{5}$

subject: Physics
q: Energy of 10 non rigid diatomic molecules at temperature $T$ is :
question: Energy of 10 non rigid diatomic molecules at temperature $T$ is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Degree of freedom }(f)=5+2(3 \mathrm{~N}-5) \\
& \qquad f=5+2(3 \times 2-1)=7 \\
& \text { energy of one molecule }=\frac{f}{2} K_B T
\end{aligned}$
energy of 10 molecules
$=10\left(\frac{\mathrm{f}}{2} \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{T}\right)=10\left(\frac{7}{2} \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{T}\right)=35 \mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{T}$

The ratio of power dissipation in first case to the total power dissipation in the second case would be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo></math> where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is
solution:
P=V2/R

Now
P1+P2P=4V2RV2R=4

q: If the root mean square velocity of hydrogen molecule at a given temperature and pressure is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> the root mean square velocity of oxygen at the same condition in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is:
question: If the root mean square velocity of hydrogen molecule at a given temperature and pressure is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> the root mean square velocity of oxygen at the same condition in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>km</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is:
solution:
Root mean square velocity is given by, Vrms=3RTM

Therefore, V1 V2=M2M1⇒2 V2=322

⇒V2=0.5 km s-1

q: Two moles of an ideal gas, with <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>, are mixed with three moles of another ideal gas <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>. The value of <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> for the mixture is
question: Two moles of an ideal gas, with <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>, are mixed with three moles of another ideal gas <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfrac></math>. The value of <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>P</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac></math> for the mixture is
solution:
Let $n_1,n_2$ be the number of moles of gases $1$ and $2$.
We have $\dfrac{C_{p_1}}{C_{v_1}}=\dfrac{5}{3}$ and We know $C_{p_1}=C_{v_1}+R$
$\therefore \dfrac{5C_{v_1}}{3}=C_{v_1}+R,\dfrac{2C_{v_1}}{3}R_1,C_{v_1}=\dfrac{3R}{2}$
$A$ and $C_{p_1}=C_{ v_ 1 }+R=\dfrac{5R}{2}$
Also, $\dfrac{C_{ p_ 2}}{C_{ v_ 2 }}=\dfrac{4}{3}$ and
$C_{p_2}=C_{v_2}+R$
$\therefore \dfrac{4C_{ v_ 2 }}{3}=C_{ v_ 2 }+R, \dfrac{C_{ v_ 2 }}{3}=R,C_{ v_ 2 }=3R$
$C_{ p_ 2}=C_{ v_ 2 }+ R=4R$
Now $C_{ v_ {mix} }=\dfrac{n_1C_{ v_ 1 }+n_2C_{ v_ 2 }}{n_1+n_2}=\dfrac{2\times \dfrac{3R}{2}+3\times 3R}{2+3}$
$=\dfrac{3R+9R}{5}=\dfrac{12R}{5}$
$C_{ p_ {mix} }=\dfrac{n_1C_{ p_ 1 }+n_2C_{ p_ 2 }}{n_1+n_2}=\dfrac{2\times \dfrac{5R}{2}+3\times 4R}{2+3}$
$=\dfrac{17R}{5}$
$l_{mix}=\dfrac{C_{ p_ {mix} }}{C_{ v_ {mix} }}=\dfrac{17}{12}=1.4166=1.42$.

solution:
f=4+3+3=10, assuming non-linear, 

β=CpCv=1+2f=1210=1.2

subject: Physics
q:
An ideal gas in a closed container is slowly heated. As its temperature increases, which of the following statements are true ? 

(A) the mean free path of the molecules decreases

(B) the mean collision time between the molecules decreases.

(C) the mean free path remains unchanged.

(D) the mean collision time relations unchanged.
question:
An ideal gas in a closed container is slowly heated. As its temperature increases, which of the following statements are true ? 

(A) the mean free path of the molecules decreases

(B) the mean collision time between the molecules decreases.

(C) the mean free path remains unchanged.

(D) the mean collision time relations unchanged.
solution:
As we know mean free path

λ=12NVπd2

N = no. of molecule

V = volume of container

d = diameter of molecule

Velocity constant and no. of molecules are same. So mean free path remains same. As temperature increases no. of collision increases so relaxation time decrease.

subject: Physics
q: The temperature of a gas having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>25</mn></msup></math> molecules per cubic meter at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>38</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math> (Given, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>38</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>23</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo></math> is :
question: The temperature of a gas having <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>25</mn></msup></math> molecules per cubic meter at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>38</mn><mo> </mo><mi>atm</mi></math> (Given, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>38</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>23</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>)</mo></math> is :
solution:
From ideal gas equation, PV=nRT

⇒PV=NNART, where N= total number of molecules

⇒P=NVRNAT⇒P=NVkT

⇒1.38×1.01×105=2×1025×1.38×10-23×T

⇒1.01×105=2×102×T

T=1.01×1032≈500 K

q: Two moles of helium are mixed with n moles of hydrogen. If <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> for the mixture then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is,
question: Two moles of helium are mixed with n moles of hydrogen. If <math><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>C</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>v</mi></mrow></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> for the mixture then the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi></math> is,
solution:
γmix=1+2fmix

CpCv= fmix+2fmix=32

⇒   fmix=4

fmix= n1f1+ nf2n1+ n2

⇒4=2×3+n×52+n

⇒n=2 mole

question: A system consists of two types of gas molecules <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> having the same number density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>25</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math>. subject: Physics
q: A cylindrical tube $A B$ of length $l$, closed at both ends contains an ideal gas of 1 mol having molecular weight $M$. The tube is rotated in a horizontal plane with constant angular velocity $\omega$ about an axis perpendicular to $A B$ and passing through the edge at end $A$, as shown in the figure. If $P_{A}$ and $P_{B}$ are the pressures at $A$ and $B$ respectively, then (Consider the temperature is same at all points in the tube)
question: A cylindrical tube $A B$ of length $l$, closed at both ends contains an ideal gas of 1 mol having molecular weight $M$. The tube is rotated in a horizontal plane with constant angular velocity $\omega$ about an axis perpendicular to $A B$ and passing through the edge at end $A$, as shown in the figure. If $P_{A}$ and $P_{B}$ are the pressures at $A$ and $B$ respectively, then (Consider the temperature is same at all points in the tube)
solution:
$A[(P + dP) - P] = (dm)(\omega^2 x)$
$dP = \dfrac{(dm)}{A} \omega^2 x$
$dP = \dfrac{(\rho)(A)(dx)\omega^2 x}{A}$
also $[PM = \rho RT]$
$\rho = \dfrac{PM}{RT}$
$dP = \left( \dfrac{PM}{RT} \right) \omega^2 x \, dx$
$\int_{P_A}^{P_B} \dfrac{dP}{P} = \dfrac{\omega^2 M}{RT} \int_{0}^{\ell} x \, dx$
$\ln \left( \dfrac{P_B}{P_A} \right) = \dfrac{\omega^2 \ell^2 M}{2RT}$
$P_B = P_A e^{\dfrac{M\omega^2 \ell^2}{2RT}}$

subject: Physics
q: One mole of ideal monoatomic gas $(\gamma=5 / 30)$ is mixed with one mole of diatomic gas $(\gamma=7 / 5)$. What is $\gamma$ for the mixture? $\gamma$ denotes the ratio of specific heat at constant pressure, to that at constant volume.
question: One mole of ideal monoatomic gas $(\gamma=5 / 30)$ is mixed with one mole of diatomic gas $(\gamma=7 / 5)$. What is $\gamma$ for the mixture? $\gamma$ denotes the ratio of specific heat at constant pressure, to that at constant volume.
solution:
$\mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{Q}_1+\mathrm{Q}_2$
$\frac{\mathrm{n}_1+\mathrm{n}_2}{\gamma_{\mathrm{m}}-1}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_1}{\gamma_1-1}+\frac{\mathrm{n}_2}{\gamma_2-1}$
$\gamma_{\mathrm{m}}=\frac{3}{2}$

question:
A 15 g mass of nitrogen gas is enclosed in a vessel at a temperature, 27oC. The amount of heat transferred to the gas, so that R.M.S. velocity of molecules is doubled, is about.
R=8.3 J K mole-1
solution:
The formula of root-mean-square speed, vrms=3RTM. For doubling the vrms, temperature, should be made 4-time is,  Tf=273+27×4 K=1200 K, the formula of, ∆Q=∆U+WW=0, 
⇒∆Q=∆U=nCV ∆T=1528×52R ∆T
⇒∆Q=1528×52×8.3×1200-300 J≈10 KJ.

q: The R.M.S. speeds of the molecules of Hydrogen, Oxygen, and Carbon dioxide at the same temperature are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></msub></math> respectively, then:
question: The R.M.S. speeds of the molecules of Hydrogen, Oxygen, and Carbon dioxide at the same temperature are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></msub><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>v</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></msub></math> respectively, then:

q: A sample contains mixture of helium and oxygen gas. The ratio of root mean square speed of helium and oxygen in the sample, is:
question: A sample contains mixture of helium and oxygen gas. The ratio of root mean square speed of helium and oxygen in the sample, is:

subject: Physics
q: Consider two boxes containing ideal gases $A$ and $B$ such that their temperatures, pressures and number densities are same. The molecular size of $A$ is half of that of $B$ and mass of molecule $A$ is four times that of $B$. If the collision frequency in gas $B$ is $32 \times 10^{18} / \mathrm{s}$ then collision frequency in gas $A$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $/ \mathrm{s}$.
question: Consider two boxes containing ideal gases $A$ and $B$ such that their temperatures, pressures and number densities are same. The molecular size of $A$ is half of that of $B$ and mass of molecule $A$ is four times that of $B$. If the collision frequency in gas $B$ is $32 \times 10^{18} / \mathrm{s}$ then collision frequency in gas $A$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $/ \mathrm{s}$.
solution:
Collision frequency $(z) = \sqrt{2} \pi d^2 N \sqrt{\dfrac{8RT}{\pi M}}$
Temp, N are same
$Z \propto \dfrac{d^2}{\sqrt{M}}$
$d_A = \dfrac{d_B}{2}$
$M_A = 4M_B$
$\dfrac{Z_A}{Z_B} = \dfrac{d_A^2}{d_B^2} \times \dfrac{\sqrt{M_B}}{\sqrt{M_A}} = \left(\sqrt{\dfrac{M_B}{M_A}}\right) \left(\dfrac{d_A}{d_B}\right)^2$
$= \left(\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{4}}\right) \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2$
$\dfrac{Z_A}{Z_B} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{4} = \dfrac{1}{8}$
$\Rightarrow Z_A = \dfrac{32 \times 10^8}{8} = 4 \times 10^8 /s$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R
Statement I: Change in internal energy of a system containing $n$ mole of ideal gas can be written as $\Delta U = n C_v (T_f - T_i) = \dfrac{nR}{\gamma - 1}(T_f - T_i)$, where $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$, $T_i =$ initial temperature, $T_f =$ final temperature.
Statement II: Relation between degree of freedom $f$ and $\gamma (= C_p/C_v)$ is $\left(\gamma = 1 + \dfrac{2}{f}\right)$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R
Statement I: Change in internal energy of a system containing $n$ mole of ideal gas can be written as $\Delta U = n C_v (T_f - T_i) = \dfrac{nR}{\gamma - 1}(T_f - T_i)$, where $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$, $T_i =$ initial temperature, $T_f =$ final temperature.
Statement II: Relation between degree of freedom $f$ and $\gamma (= C_p/C_v)$ is $\left(\gamma = 1 + \dfrac{2}{f}\right)$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true
solution:
Statement I is true because for an ideal gas, the change in internal energy is given by $\Delta U = n C_v \Delta T$. Using Mayer's relation $C_p - C_v = R$ and the ratio of specific heats $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v}$, we can write $C_v = \dfrac{R}{\gamma - 1}$. Substituting this, we get $\Delta U = \dfrac{nR}{\gamma - 1}(T_f - T_i)$.

Statement II is true because according to the law of equipartition of energy, the molar heat capacity at constant volume is $C_v = \dfrac{f}{2}R$ and at constant pressure is $C_p = \left(\dfrac{f}{2} + 1\right)R$. Therefore, $\gamma = \dfrac{C_p}{C_v} = 1 + \dfrac{2}{f}$.

Statement II does not explain Statement I, as Statement I is derived purely from macroscopic thermodynamic relations (Mayer's relation) and is independent of the microscopic concept of degrees of freedom.

Hence, both statements are true but Statement II is NOT the correct explanation of Statement I.

Answer: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A

question: The mean free path of molecules of a certain gas at STP is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1500</mn><mi>d</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi><mo> </mo></math>is the diameter of the gas molecules. The mean free path is given by the relation,

λ=RT2πd2NAP

So, λ∝T

Let λ' be the new mean free path.

Hence,

1500dλ'=273373

⇒λ'=2049.45d≈2049d

subject: Physics
q:
A mixture of carbon dioxide and oxygen has volume $8310$ cm$^3$, temperature $300$ K, pressure $100$ kPa and mass $13.2$ g. The number of moles of carbon dioxide and oxygen gases in the mixture respectively are _______.
(Assume both carbon dioxide and oxygen gases behave like ideal gases) $[R = 8.31$ J/mol.K$]$
question:
A mixture of carbon dioxide and oxygen has volume $8310$ cm$^3$, temperature $300$ K, pressure $100$ kPa and mass $13.2$ g. The number of moles of carbon dioxide and oxygen gases in the mixture respectively are _______.
(Assume both carbon dioxide and oxygen gases behave like ideal gases) $[R = 8.31$ J/mol.K$]$
solution:
Using the ideal gas equation, the total number of moles of the mixture is given by:

$n = \dfrac{PV}{RT}$

Substituting the given values:
$P = 100 \text{ kPa} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
$V = 8310 \text{ cm}^3 = 8.31 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$
$T = 300 \text{ K}$
$R = 8.31 \text{ J/(mol}\cdot\text{K)}$

$n = \dfrac{10^5 \times 8.31 \times 10^{-3}}{8.31 \times 300} = \dfrac{100}{300} = \dfrac{1}{3} \text{ mol}$

Let $n_1$ and $n_2$ be the number of moles of $CO_2$ and $O_2$ respectively.

$n_1 + n_2 = \dfrac{1}{3}$

The total mass of the mixture is $13.2 \text{ g}$. Using the molar masses of $CO_2$ ($44 \text{ g/mol}$) and $O_2$ ($32 \text{ g/mol}$):

$44n_1 + 32n_2 = 13.2$

Substituting $n_2 = \dfrac{1}{3} - n_1$ into the mass equation:

$44n_1 + 32\left(\dfrac{1}{3} - n_1\right) = 13.2$

$12n_1 + \dfrac{32}{3} = 13.2$

$12n_1 = \dfrac{39.6 - 32}{3} = \dfrac{7.6}{3}$

$n_1 = \dfrac{7.6}{36} = \dfrac{19}{90} \approx 0.21 \text{ mol}$

The number of moles of $O_2$ is:

$n_2 = \dfrac{1}{3} - \dfrac{19}{90} = \dfrac{11}{90} \approx 0.12 \text{ mol}$

The number of moles of $CO_2$ and $O_2$ are $0.21$ and $0.12$ respectively.

Answer: $0.21$ and $0.12$
